steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome to the MCP Maximizer lab! In this hands-on session you will build a production-grade MCP server from scratch, register typed tools, wrap internal APIs, add authentication, containerize for deployment, and configure monitoring.

This lab assumes you have completed the Beginner MCP course and are comfortable with MCP concepts, VS Code, and TypeScript or Python. The guide panel will update as you advance through the slides.

Note: All lab exercises use TypeScript. Python and C# developers should follow along conceptually and adapt the patterns to the mcp Python SDK or the ModelContextProtocol .NET package respectively.

steps.title: Scaffold the MCP Server Project
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and create a new directory for your MCP server:

mkdir mcp-inventory-server && cd mcp-inventory-server

Initialize the project:

npm init -y

steps.title: Install the MCP SDK
steps.instruction:
Install the official TypeScript SDK and Zod for schema validation:

npm install @modelcontextprotocol/sdk zod

Then install TypeScript as a dev dependency:

npm install -D typescript @types/node tsx

steps.title: Create the Server Entry Point
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and ask it to scaffold the server file:

Create a file src/index.ts with a minimal MCP server using @modelcontextprotocol/sdk. Name the server "inventory-service" version "1.0.0". Use the stdio transport. Register one placeholder tool called "ping" that returns "pong".

Review the generated code. It should match the pattern shown on the slide: import McpServer, create an instance, register a tool, connect via StdioServerTransport.

steps.title: Check the Project Structure
steps.instruction:
Open Explorer to review the generated project layout. You should see:

• package.json with MCP SDK and Zod dependencies
• src/index.ts with the server entry point
• node_modules/ with installed packages

A clean project structure from the start makes enterprise maintenance easier.

steps.title: Verify the Server Starts
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal again. Add a start script to your package.json:

"start": "tsx src/index.ts"

Then test that the server starts without errors by running:

npx tsx src/index.ts

The process should start and wait for stdio input. Press Ctrl+C to stop it.
steps.tip: If you see import errors, make sure your package.json has "type": "module" set.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E

steps.title: Design Your Tool Set
steps.instruction:
Before writing code, plan the tools your inventory server will expose. In Copilot Chat, ask:

Help me design 4 MCP tools for an inventory management server: lookup_product, search_products, check_stock, and update_stock_level. For each tool, give me the name, description, and input schema following MCP best practices: verb_noun naming, single responsibility, rich descriptions, and typed parameters.

Review the output against the patterns on the slide: single responsibility, descriptive naming, explicit mutations.

steps.title: Register the Tools
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to generate the tool registrations in your server file:

In src/index.ts, register these 4 tools using server.tool() with Zod schemas: lookup_product (takes sku string), search_products (takes query string, category optional enum, limit number max 50 default 10), check_stock (takes sku string, warehouse optional string), update_stock_level (takes sku string, quantity number, reason string). Use mock data for now.

Verify that each tool follows the patterns from the slide: typed schemas, rich descriptions, idempotent reads, and explicit mutation descriptions.
steps.tip: The update_stock_level tool modifies state. Its description should clearly say so, e.g., "Adjust the stock quantity for a product. This is a write operation."

steps.title: Check for TypeScript Errors
steps.instruction:
Open the Problems panel to check for any TypeScript errors after registering your tools.

Look for type mismatches in Zod schemas, missing imports, or incorrect parameter types. Fixing these now prevents runtime failures when Copilot calls your tools.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I
• Problems Panel: Ctrl+Shift+M

steps.title: Add Error Handling
steps.instruction:
Your tools need robust error handling before they are production-ready. Ask Copilot to refactor:

Refactor lookup_product in src/index.ts to add proper error handling: wrap the handler in try-catch, return isError: true with a descriptive message when the product is not found, and return isError: true with a sanitized error message if an exception occurs. Follow the MCP error pattern from the course.

Compare the result to the code example on the slide. Ensure it returns structured isError: true responses rather than throwing exceptions.

steps.title: Apply Error Handling to All Tools
steps.instruction:
Extend the pattern to the remaining tools. Ask Copilot:

Apply the same error handling pattern (try-catch, isError responses, sanitized messages) to search_products, check_stock, and update_stock_level in src/index.ts.

Review each tool to confirm:

• Schema validation catches malformed input before the handler runs
• Not-found cases return isError: true with a clear message
• Exceptions are caught and returned as structured errors
• No unhandled exceptions can crash the server

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I

steps.title: Wrap a REST API
steps.instruction:
Now replace the mock data with a real API call pattern. Ask Copilot:

Refactor lookup_product in src/index.ts to call an external REST API. Use fetch to call "${INVENTORY_API_BASE}/products/${sku}" with an Authorization Bearer header from an environment variable INVENTORY_API_KEY. Parse the JSON response and return the product data. Handle HTTP errors (non-200 status codes) as isError responses.

This pattern transforms your MCP server from a demo into a real enterprise integration.

steps.title: Add Environment Configuration
steps.instruction:
Create a configuration module to centralize environment variables. Ask Copilot:

Create src/config.ts that exports INVENTORY_API_BASE and INVENTORY_API_KEY from process.env with validation. Throw a clear error at startup if required variables are missing. Import and use it in src/index.ts.

This follows the enterprise pattern of failing fast with clear messages when configuration is missing.
steps.tip: Never hardcode API keys or secrets in source code. Always use environment variables or a secret manager.

steps.title: Test With a .vscode/mcp.json Configuration
steps.instruction:
Create a .vscode/mcp.json file in your workspace to register the server with VS Code:

Create a .vscode/mcp.json file that configures my inventory-service MCP server using stdio transport. The command should be "npx" with args ["tsx", "src/index.ts"]. Set env variables INVENTORY_API_BASE and INVENTORY_API_KEY to placeholder values.

steps.title: Verify MCP Server Connection
steps.instruction:
With your .vscode/mcp.json configured, VS Code discovers and starts the server automatically.

Open Copilot Chat (Ctrl+Alt+I), click the Tools button, and confirm your inventory-service tools appear in the list. VS Code will show a trust dialog the first time a tool is invoked — approve it to grant the server permission to run.

For reference, open Settings (Ctrl+,) and search mcp to see available MCP-related settings. The key setting is chat.mcp.autoStart, which controls whether servers configured in .vscode/mcp.json start automatically when VS Code loads.

steps.title: Reload and Discover MCP Commands
steps.instruction:
Open the Command Palette and type MCP to see available MCP commands.

Look for MCP: List Servers to verify your server is registered, and MCP: Add Server to understand how servers can be added interactively. These commands are the fastest way to manage MCP connections in VS Code.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I
• Settings: Ctrl+,
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P

steps.title: Add a Database Query Tool
steps.instruction:
Following the safe database access patterns from the previous slide, add a database tool to your server. Ask Copilot:

Add a tool called "query_inventory_report" to src/index.ts that queries a PostgreSQL database. Use parameterized queries with placeholders ($1, $2, $3). Accept parameters: warehouse (string), start_date (string YYYY-MM-DD), end_date (string YYYY-MM-DD), limit (number, max 100, default 25). The query should SELECT product_sku, product_name, quantity, last_updated FROM inventory WHERE warehouse = $1 AND last_updated BETWEEN $2 AND $3 ORDER BY last_updated DESC LIMIT $4.

steps.title: Review the Safety Checklist
steps.instruction:
Review your database tool against the safety checklist from the previous slide:

• Is the database connection read-only? (SELECT only)
• Are all queries parameterized? (No string interpolation)
• Is there a hard row limit? (max 100 in schema)
• Does error handling prevent leaking internal details?

If any check fails, refactor the tool before moving on. Production database access demands defense in depth.
steps.tip: In production, create a dedicated database user with SELECT-only permissions. Never reuse a write-capable connection for MCP queries.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I

steps.title: Add Token-Based Authentication
steps.instruction:
Your server needs to authenticate with downstream APIs securely. Ask Copilot:

Create src/auth.ts that implements a getToken() function. It should support two modes controlled by an AUTH_MODE environment variable: "api-key" mode returns the value of API_KEY env var directly, and "oauth" mode uses client credentials flow to POST to a TOKEN_ENDPOINT with CLIENT_ID and CLIENT_SECRET, caching the access token until it expires.

Import and use getToken() in your tool handlers instead of reading environment variables directly.

steps.title: Implement Token Caching
steps.instruction:
Review the generated src/auth.ts file. Verify that the OAuth path:

• Caches the access token in memory
• Checks expires_in and refreshes before expiry
• Does not log or expose the token value
• Returns a clear error if token acquisition fails

If the caching logic is missing or incomplete, ask Copilot to add it. Token caching prevents your server from hitting the token endpoint on every tool call.
steps.tip: For Azure deployments, consider using Managed Identity via @azure/identity instead of client credentials. It eliminates secrets entirely.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I

steps.title: Create a Dockerfile
steps.instruction:
Containerize your MCP server for remote deployment. Ask Copilot:

Create a Dockerfile for my TypeScript MCP server. Use a multi-stage build: first stage compiles TypeScript, second stage uses node:22-slim for the runtime image. Copy only the compiled output and production node_modules. Expose port 3000. Set NODE_ENV=production. The CMD should run the compiled server with Streamable HTTP transport.

steps.title: Add Streamable HTTP Transport
steps.instruction:
For remote deployment, your server needs to use Streamable HTTP instead of stdio. Ask Copilot:

Update src/index.ts to support both transports. If the environment variable TRANSPORT is set to "http", use StreamableHTTPServerTransport on port 3000 with an Express server. Otherwise, default to StdioServerTransport. Add a /health endpoint that returns 200 OK.

This pattern lets you run the same server locally (stdio) and remotely (HTTP) without code changes.

steps.title: Review Changes in Source Control
steps.instruction:
Before containerizing, open Source Control to review everything you have built so far.

You should see src/index.ts, src/config.ts, src/auth.ts, package.json, and the new Dockerfile. Review the diffs to verify no hardcoded secrets, proper error handling, and clean code structure. This review checkpoint mirrors a real enterprise workflow.

steps.title: Test the Docker Build
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal and build the container image locally to verify it compiles:

docker build -t mcp-inventory-server .

If the build succeeds, test it:

docker run -p 3000:3000 -e TRANSPORT=http -e INVENTORY_API_BASE=https://example.com -e INVENTORY_API_KEY=test mcp-inventory-server

Verify the /health endpoint returns 200 by opening a new terminal and running curl http://localhost:3000/health.
steps.tip: If Docker is not available in your environment, reviewing the Dockerfile and transport code is sufficient for this exercise.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I
• Source Control: Ctrl+Shift+G
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Test Tool Chaining
steps.instruction:
With your MCP server connected, test Copilot's ability to chain your tools together. Open Copilot Chat and try a multi-step prompt:

Search for products in the "electronics" category, then check the stock level for the first result in the "US-WEST" warehouse. If stock is below 10, update it to 50 with reason "restock".

Watch how Copilot calls search_products, then check_stock, then conditionally calls update_stock_level. Each tool returns context that informs the next step.

steps.title: Observe the Confirmation Flow
steps.instruction:
Notice how Copilot handles the write operation. When it reaches update_stock_level, it should show a confirmation dialog before executing.

Review the tool call sequence in the chat output. Verify that:

• Read tools (search, check_stock) executed without confirmation
• The write tool (update_stock_level) prompted for approval
• Each tool's output provided enough context for the next call

This is the composable building block pattern in action.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I

steps.title: Add Structured Logging
steps.instruction:
Production servers need observability. Ask Copilot:

Create src/logger.ts that exports a log function. Each log entry should be a JSON object with: timestamp (ISO 8601), level (info/warn/error), tool_name, duration_ms, status (success/error), and a sanitized params object that redacts any field containing "key", "token", or "secret". Wire it into every tool handler in src/index.ts to log each invocation.

steps.title: Add a Health Endpoint with Metrics
steps.instruction:
Extend the /health endpoint to return basic metrics. Ask Copilot:

Update the health endpoint in src/index.ts to return JSON with: status "ok", uptime in seconds, total_invocations count, error_count, and active_connections. Track these counters in a simple in-memory metrics object that increments on each tool call.

This gives operations teams a quick way to verify the server is healthy and see basic usage data.

steps.title: Verify Logging Output
steps.instruction:
Open the Terminal, run your server locally, and invoke a tool through Copilot Chat. Check the terminal output for structured log entries. Each entry should look like:

{"timestamp":"...","level":"info","tool_name":"lookup_product","duration_ms":42,"status":"success"}

Verify that sensitive values are redacted in the params field. If any secrets appear in the logs, fix the sanitization logic before proceeding.

steps.title: Check MCP Server Output Logs
steps.instruction:
Open the Output panel and look for your MCP server's channel in the dropdown. Select it to view the server's structured log output.

Compare what you see in Output with the terminal logs. The Output panel shows the MCP protocol-level communication between VS Code and your server — tool invocations, responses, and errors. This is the primary debugging surface for MCP servers in production.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Output Panel: Ctrl+Shift+U

steps.title: Plan a Complete Integration
steps.instruction:
Follow the seven-step process on the slide to plan a new MCP integration for a system your team uses (e.g., Jira, ServiceNow, Confluence, or an internal service). Open a new file and document:

• Workflow: What does the developer need to accomplish?
• Tools: What discrete operations are needed?
• Schemas: What are the input parameters for each tool?

Use Copilot Chat to brainstorm:

Help me design an MCP server for [your system]. I need to support [your workflow]. What tools should I create?

steps.title: Generate the Server Skeleton
steps.instruction:
Use Copilot to generate the full server skeleton from your design. Ask:

Generate a complete MCP server in TypeScript for the tools we just designed. Include Zod schemas, error handling with isError, structured logging, and support for both stdio and Streamable HTTP transports. Use the patterns from our inventory-service server.

Review the generated code. Verify it follows all the enterprise patterns covered in this course: typed schemas, error handling, authentication, and logging.

steps.title: Configure and Test
steps.instruction:
Add the new server to your .vscode/mcp.json alongside the inventory server. Reload VS Code (Ctrl+Shift+P then Reload Window).

Open Copilot Chat and verify both servers' tools appear in the tools list. Try a prompt that chains tools from both servers:

Look up product SKU-1234 in the inventory system, then create a Jira ticket for restocking if the quantity is below threshold.

This demonstrates MCP's composability: multiple servers working together in a single workflow.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the Chat panel for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! You have completed the MCP Maximizer lab. Here is what you built:

• Scaffolded a TypeScript MCP server from scratch using the official SDK
• Designed and registered typed tools following enterprise best practices
• Added robust error handling with structured isError responses
• Wrapped REST APIs as MCP tools with environment-based configuration
• Built a safe database query tool with parameterized queries and row limits
• Implemented token-based authentication with caching
• Containerized the server with Docker and added Streamable HTTP transport
• Tested multi-tool chaining and observed the confirmation flow
• Added structured logging, metrics, and a health endpoint
• Planned and generated a complete integration from scratch

Next up: Multi-Agent Multi-Repo Roundup to orchestrate agents across repositories.
